title: Dog Facial Drooping: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticing one side of your dog's face drooping? Learn about facial nerve paralysis, urgency levels, and how to help your vet with diagnosis.
content_html:
Understanding Facial Drooping in Dogs

If you have noticed that one side of your dog's face looks "heavy" or is sagging, they are likely experiencing facial nerve paralysis. This condition occurs when the nerve responsible for controlling the muscles of the face—including the lips, eyelids, and ears—is damaged or inflamed. You might notice your dog is unable to blink, their ear hangs lower than usual, or food and water fall out of the side of their mouth.

Is This an Emergency?

Facial drooping is generally classified as a Medium Urgency situation. While it is rarely an immediate life-threat, your dog should be examined by a veterinarian within 24 hours to identify the underlying cause. However, if the drooping is accompanied by severe balance issues, an inability to stand, or extreme lethargy, you should seek emergency care immediately, as these could be signs of a more serious neurological event.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo or a short video of your dog's face can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. Facial drooping can sometimes be subtle or fluctuate depending on the dog's activity level. A visual record allows the vet to see exactly what you are seeing at home, helps them assess the severity of the paralysis, and provides a baseline to track whether the condition is improving or worsening over time.

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from facial paralysis?
faqs.answer: Many dogs recover partially or fully depending on the underlying cause, such as an ear infection or hypothyroidism. In some idiopathic cases, the drooping may be permanent but manageable.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of facial drooping?
faqs.answer: Common causes include middle or inner ear infections, trauma to the head, endocrine diseases like hypothyroidism, or it can be idiopathic, meaning the cause is unknown.

faqs.question: How can I help my dog eat if their face is drooping?
faqs.answer: Since dogs with facial paralysis often lose the ability to keep food in their mouths, you can try hand-feeding or using a shallower, wider bowl to make it easier for them to scoop up their food.
